channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Research Funding Slows Again for Universities Targeted by White House
item.description: The National Science Foundation lifted a hold on some grants for Harvard and other universities this week after inquiries from media outlets, including The New York Times.
item.dc:creator: Adam Sella, Mark Arsenault, Stephanie Saul and Michael C. Bender
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Harvard has faced more government actions than any school since President Trump returned to office.

item.title: Trump Clears Way for Companies to Avoid Taxes in Havens Including Malta and Cyprus
item.description: U.S. companies skirted at least $40 billion in taxes since the beginning of 2025 thanks to schemes in places like Malta, Bermuda and Cyprus.
item.dc:creator: Jesse Drucker and Dylan Freedman
item.media:credit: Susan Wright for The New York Times
item.media:description: A favorite destination for tax-dodging by U.S. companies was the tiny Mediterranean island of Malta.

item.title: ICE Agent Wanted in Minneapolis Shooting Is Captured in Texas
item.description: Christian Castro, who had been charged with assault in the shooting of a Venezuelan man during the immigration crackdown in Minnesota, was taken into custody Friday morning.
item.dc:creator: Ernesto Londoño
item.media:credit: David Guttenfelder/The New York Times
item.media:description: Federal agents near the scene of the shooting in Minneapolis in January.

item.title: In Carroll Lawsuits Inquiry, Scrutiny Turns Toward Private Citizens Who Antagonized Trump
item.description: The Justice Department is said to be examining the funding of lawsuits brought by E. Jean Carroll, an author who has never sought a public role, political power or governmental authority.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush and Benjamin Weiser
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: E. Jean Carroll, the author and columnist, leaving court in Manhattan in 2024 after a hearing on one of her lawsuits against Donald J. Trump.

item.title: Where Do E. Jean Carroll’s Lawsuits Against President Trump Stand?
item.description: Two monetary judgments against Mr. Trump are winding their way through the legal system, with the Supreme Court likely to be the final word on both.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Jefferson Siegel for The New York Times
item.media:description: E. Jean Carroll leaves court after closing arguments in her defamation trial against Donald Trump in 2023.

item.title: Inside the Room Where War Insurance Is Bought and Sold
item.description: Many of the ships stranded in the Persian Gulf depend on coverage negotiated at Lloyd’s, the center of marine insurance for more than 300 years.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Joseph Horton for The New York Times
item.media:description: The 200-foot-tall atrium in the Underwriting Room, where brokers negotiate policies for ships carrying oil, gas and other goods around the world.

item.title: Israeli Troops Push Deeper Into Lebanon as Cease-Fire Talks Continue
item.description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said Israeli ground forces had crossed Lebanon’s Litani River, as military officials from the two countries were set to meet for U.S.-brokered talks.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Daniel Berehulak/The New York Times
item.media:description: An apartment building in Tyre, Lebanon, on Tuesday, in the aftermath of an Israeli strike.

item.title: Louisiana Approves Map Eliminating a Majority-Black District
item.description: A Supreme Court court ruling last month rejected the state’s previous congressional map as an illegal racial gerrymander and set off a redistricting race across the South.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane
item.media:credit: Gerald Herbert/Associated Press, via Associated Press Photo, via Gerald Herbert
item.media:description: State Representative Edmond Jordan of Louisiana speaks with fellow lawmakers before a Louisiana House vote on a redistricting plan to eliminate a majority-Black congressional district in response to a U.S. Supreme Court ruling, in Baton Rouge, La., on Thursday.

item.title: The Politics of the Downwardly Mobile Professional Class
item.description: The debate over whether Graham Platner is “working class” comes at a time when more and more people are at risk of falling into it.
item.dc:creator: Noam Scheiber

item.title: Times/Siena Poll: 43% of Voters Dissatisfied With Both Parties
item.description: Persistent frustration over the economy and foreign policy has left many Americans feeling politically homeless, and young voters are particularly frustrated.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Igielnik
item.media:credit: Michael Swensen for The New York Times
item.media:description: The poll found that just 26 percent of voters felt satisfied with the Democratic Party and that 33 percent felt satisfied with the Republican Party.

item.title: California House Primary in Sacramento Displays Democrats’ Fierce Generational Battle
item.description: Representative Doris Matsui has been re-elected 10 times with ease. But an upstart Democrat is threatening to end her run in Congress.
item.dc:creator: Laurel Rosenhall
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Doris Matsui is facing her toughest re-election fight in her more than two decades of representing a safely Democratic district in the Sacramento region.

item.title: Russian Drone Hits Romanian Apartment Building, Officials Say
item.description: Romania is a NATO country, and the security alliance condemned “Russia’s recklessness” for an episode that sharply escalated tensions with Moscow.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Higgins and Cassandra Vinograd
item.media:credit: Inquam Photos, via Reuters
item.media:description: The site where a drone hit a building in Galati, Romania, on Friday, close to the border with Ukraine.

item.title: What to Know About the Drones That Have Been Crossing Into Romania
item.description: The strike on Friday, near the Ukrainian border, was the first to injure civilians in Romania, officials say. But drones from the nearby war have been a growing threat.
item.dc:creator: Lara Jakes
item.media:credit: Daniel Mihailescu/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Investigators on the roof of the apartment building in Galati, Romania, that was struck on Friday by what officials called a Russian drone.

item.title: Romania Says It Could Invoke NATO’s Article 4. What Would That Do?
item.description: The clause in NATO’s founding treaty is an “instrument” Romania can use, the foreign minister said after a drone, alleged to be Russian, wounded two civilians.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross and Aurelien Breeden
item.media:credit: Daniel Mihailescu/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Investigators at the site of a drone crash at an apartment building in Galati, Romania, on Friday.

item.title: As the SpaceX Hype Machine Steamrolls Ahead, Wall Street Jumps Aboard
item.description: Elon Musk and his bankers are working to create a self-fulfilling prophecy for the massive public offering: Make investors feel that the risks of passing it up outweigh those of buying into it.
item.dc:creator: Maureen Farrell and Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: SpaceX’s launch site near Boca Chica, Texas, in 2024. The company could go public as soon as next month.

item.title: Blue Origin Rocket Blows Up on Florida Launchpad During Test
item.description: The rocket, built by the Jeff Bezos-owned space company, was to carry 48 satellites into space. Blue Origin reported on social media that “all personnel have been accounted for.”
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:credit: Malcolm Denemark/USA Today, via Imagn Images, via Reuters. Video by Nailah Morgan
item.media:description: The Blue Origin launchpad seen in flames from Cocoa Beach, Fla. on Thursday night.

item.title: Sky-High I.P.O. Pricing Isn’t Great for Real People
item.description: When newly public companies have been valued as richly as SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic seem likely to be, the outlook for ordinary investors has been poor.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Sommer
item.media:credit: Eric Gay/Associated Press
item.media:description: SpaceX’s Starship rocket during a test flight in May. The company’s chief executive, Elon Musk, stands to unlock a great fortune when the company goes public.

item.title: A Hidden Treasure of Rare Snake Specimens
item.description: In the foothills of the Ecuadorean Amazon, a 101-year-old farmer and a young scientist turned an amateur collection into a scientific survey of one of the most diverse snake habitats on Earth.
item.dc:creator: Alexa Robles-Gil and Johanna Alarcón
item.media:description: Manuel Genaro Peñafiel Flores with some of his collection of snakes at home in Mera, Ecuador.

item.title: The Knicks’ Playoff Run Has Brought a Peculiar Harmony to New York
item.description: The team’s playoff run has brought a curious sense of amity to the city.
item.dc:creator: Matt Flegenheimer
item.media:credit: José A. Alvarado Jr. for The New York Times
item.media:description: A blazing playoff run has electrified New York Knicks fans of all ages, in a city unaccustomed to such things from its basketball team.

item.title: Luka Doncic on Trying to Bring an N.B.A. Team to Rome
item.description: The superstar is part of group that has bought a basketball team in Italy, with a plan to move the club to Rome in hopes of making it part of a new league.
item.dc:creator: Tania Ganguli
item.media:credit: Gerald Leong/Associated Press
item.media:description: “Since I came to the N.B.A., my dream was always to own a team in Europe,” said Luka Doncic, a Slovenian basketball star who plays for the Los Angeles Lakers.

item.title: Is Spencer Pratt for Real?
item.description: The former reality star’s dark visions of Los Angeles are resonating in a heated mayoral race, even if they’re far from the truth.
item.dc:creator: Nathan Taylor Pemberton
item.media:credit: Alex Welsh for The New York Times
item.media:description: “It’s not a kind of fame that is fun,” Spencer Pratt said of his political turn.

item.title: Spencer Pratt Wants to Be Mayor. His Fans Want Him to Be Batman.
item.description: What an A.I.-generated video of the former reality TV star teaches us about the future of political ads.
item.dc:creator: Willy Staley

item.title: A.I. Doesn’t Have to Mean Layoffs
item.description: A French multinational, Schneider Electric, decided to use artificial intelligence in manufacturing to make workers more productive, rather than to replace them. Here’s how that’s going.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Cohen and Alexis Pazoumian

item.title: The $900 Billion Giant: How Anthropic Got So Big, So Fast
item.description: The artificial intelligence giant was just valued at $900 billion, surpassing OpenAI. Here are the numbers behind its rise — and headwinds it faces.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe, Ian Mount and Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Anthropic, which created the Claude A.I. model, has surged in popularity with users, pushing its valuation to $900 billion.

item.title: No, Not That Georgia. A.I. Compounds a Nation’s Identity Woes.
item.description: Online queries are more likely to turn up references to the U.S. state — a problem for the republic in the Caucasus and its people. But a move to drop the name “Georgia” has lost steam.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Nechepurenko
item.media:credit: Tako Robakidze for The New York Times
item.media:description: Georgia’s ancient, distinctive language and script have long made up part of its national identity.

item.title: Uber to Offer Shuttles for Fans Leaving World Cup Matches
item.description: Soccer lovers frustrated by public transportation will have another option to return to Boston, Dallas, Miami and New York from hard-to-reach stadiums.
item.dc:creator: Christine Chung
item.media:credit: Rob Carr/Getty Images
item.media:description: Uber’s shared shuttles will take fans back to Manhattan from MetLife Stadium for $49.

item.title: ‘Backrooms’ Is Part of a Boomlet in Movies From YouTube Creators
item.description: “Backrooms,” a psychological horror flick opening this weekend, is part of a wave of breakout films from fledgling directors who honed their instincts on YouTube.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.media:credit: Asterios Moutsokapas/A24, via Associated Press
item.media:description: The director of “Backrooms,” Kane Parsons, left, on a set with one of the new film’s stars, Chiwetel Ejiofor.

item.title: Kane Parsons: From YouTube Sensation to A24’s Youngest Director
item.description: Now 20, the viral creator Kane Parsons is releasing “Backrooms,” a feature-length expansion of his web series.
item.dc:creator: Kyle Buchanan
item.media:credit: Kobe Wagstaff for The New York Times
item.media:description: “Age has never not been part of the conversation with me,” Parsons said.

item.title: Hungary Showed How to Defeat an Autocrat
item.description: Eleven lessons America must learn
item.dc:creator: M. Gessen and Máté Bartha
item.media:credit: Máté Bartha for The New York Times

item.title: America Has a Masculinity Crisis
item.description: A much-needed, nuanced conversation about masculinity and feminism today.
item.dc:creator: Nadja Spiegelman, Frederick Joseph, Ruth Whippman and Vishakha Darbha
item.media:credit: Illustration by The New York Times; photo by Raul Arboleta/Getty

item.title: Pool Problems
item.description: What’s happening with the paint on the National Mall?
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: In Washington.

item.title: After Orban’s Defeat, Hungary Is Poised to Get Billions From E.U.
item.description: The European Union is set to release 16 billion euros it had withheld, as Hungary moves to enact anti-corruption and rule of law policies.
item.dc:creator: Lara Jakes
item.media:credit: John Moore/Getty Images
item.media:description: Budapest, Hungary, in early May.

item.title: Woman With No Right Hand Is Pulled Over for Using Phone in Her Right Hand
item.description: Her case was, of course, dismissed.
item.dc:creator: Mark Walker

item.title: One of Five Gold Miners Is Rescued From Laos Cave After More Than a Week
item.description: An international effort to free the trapped men brought attention to the country’s informal mines, where panning for gold can pay more than minimum wage.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Regalado and Muktita Suhartono
item.media:credit: Association Of Volunteers For Lao People, via Associated Press
